map.subtitle: Unit 2: Securing Spaces   •   5 days   •   LO 2.2.A, 2.2.B, 2.2.C
map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–5 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–5 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Physical Attack Identifier and Exercise 2: Xtensr Vulnerability Advisor (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Securing the Space (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 4)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 28 questions, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 names the attacks, Day 2 how threats exploit vulnerabilities, Day 3 the risk method, Day 4 the Scenario 2A Xtensr assessment, Day 5 review and quiz).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.
Naming: the CED topic is 'Physical Vulnerabilities'; the CED scenario is 'Scenario 2A: Securing Xtensr Labs'; the unit hub lists this lesson under Unit 2, 'Securing Spaces.' All refer to this lesson.

exercise1.title: Physical Attack Identifier
exercise1.intro: You are a physical-security analyst. Five incidents from this week's surveillance and access logs are below. For each one you will name the CB physical attack, cite its EK, and identify the social-engineering or access mechanism behind it. Part A works the incident log; Part B extends the same skills to how physical access turns into loss. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives 2.2.A and 2.2.B.
exercise1.narrative: Your security operations center logged five events at the office campus this week. Each row records the location, what surveillance or witnesses observed, what the authorized employee did or experienced, and the after-effect. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, find the defining action (manipulated into granting access, followed unnoticed, watched, trash harvested, or card copied), then match the attack name and EK to that mechanism.

rows: Badge door, loading dock
A person in a delivery uniform held a large, heavy box in both hands and nodded toward the door.
Badged in, saw the box, and held the door open so the person could walk through.
An unauthorized person was now inside the restricted area.
Main security door
An adversary timed their arrival to slip through the slowly closing door a half-second behind an employee.
Looked at their phone and never noticed anyone came in behind them.
An unauthorized person reached the inner offices undetected.
Keypad outside secure lab
A small camera angled at the keypad recorded employees entering door codes over several days.
Typed their door code normally, unaware it was being recorded.
The adversary planned to analyze the footage later to learn the codes.
Curbside trash bins
After hours, an adversary pulled discarded printouts, sticky notes, and an old org chart out of the bins.
Nothing — the papers had been thrown away that afternoon.
Usernames and internal contacts were exposed without anyone entering the building.
Crowded elevator
An adversary skimmed the data from an employee's proximity badge and wrote it to a blank card.
Rode the elevator normally; the badge never left their pocket.
The blank card later opened every door the employee could open, while the original kept working.

exercise1.scenarioNote: Incidents adapted from the Day 1 named-attack examples and the Lesson 2.2 quiz scenarios; attack names and EKs follow the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: of the five incidents, which two are the easiest to confuse, and what single detail tells them apart?

partA.q: Incident 1 (loading dock) and Incident 2 (security door) both end with an unauthorized person inside, yet they are two different CB attacks. Name each attack with its EK, quote the defining action that proves it, and state the one question that separates them.
partA.answer: Incident 1 is piggybacking (EK 2.2.A.2): the employee KNOWINGLY held the door open after being manipulated by the heavy box and uniform — carrying something large to entice a person to hold the door is a named CB tactic. Incident 2 is tailgating (EK 2.2.A.3): the adversary followed close behind WITHOUT the employee's awareness or knowledge — the employee on their phone never noticed. The separating question: did the authorized person knowingly grant access? Yes = piggybacking; no = tailgating.

partA.q: Incident 3 (keypad camera) is sometimes mislabeled. Name the correct CB attack and EK, and explain how the camera detail fits the official definition rather than turning it into a different attack.
partA.answer: Incident 3 is shoulder surfing (EK 2.2.A.4): the adversary watches as a user accesses sensitive information so it can be used later. The CED explicitly notes that adversaries sometimes use a camera to record the target accessing the information for later analysis — so recording the keypad over several days is still shoulder surfing, not a new attack. No restricted area was entered; the credential was stolen by observation.

partA.q: Incident 4 (trash bins) and Incident 3 (keypad camera) both steal information without entering the building. Name Incident 4's attack and EK, and explain the source difference that distinguishes the two info-stealing attacks.
partA.answer: Incident 4 is dumpster diving (EK 2.2.A.5): the adversary goes through a target's physical trash for information that could help reach their goal — the printouts, sticky notes, and org chart. The source difference: shoulder surfing watches a LIVE access (eyes/camera on a screen or keypad), while dumpster diving harvests DISCARDED material (paper in the trash). Name the source — live access vs. trash — to tell them apart.

partA.q: Incident 5 (elevator) is dangerous even though the employee never lost their badge. Name the attack and EK, and explain why the original cardholder noticing nothing is exactly what makes the attack effective.
partA.answer: Incident 5 is card cloning (EK 2.2.A.6): the adversary makes a copy of an authorized user's access card to gain access to all the resources that user is authorized to access. Because the original badge keeps working normally, nothing looks wrong to the employee — the clone grants the adversary full access while the legitimate user has no signal that their credential was duplicated, so the breach can persist unnoticed.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Suppose the adversary from Incident 5 uses the cloned card to reach a server protected by a strong password and a firewall. Explain, citing EK 2.2.C.1, why those digital controls do not protect the machine, and name one thing the adversary could now do per EK 2.2.B.5.
partB.answer: EK 2.2.C.1 states that physical access to devices can allow adversaries to bypass many technical controls and layers of security — once the adversary is standing at the machine, the password and firewall no longer stand between them and the hardware. Per EK 2.2.B.5 they could plug a keylogger or a malware-loaded external drive into an open port to collect data or gain control of the device, or physically destroy the device to make it and its services unavailable.

partB.q: Every incident in the log involves a human adversary. Per EK 2.2.B.1, name the other category of threat to physical security and describe two harms it can cause that a human adversary also causes.
partB.answer: Per EK 2.2.B.1 the other category is natural disasters. Like a human adversary, a natural disaster can cause physical damage or destruction to computers and data, and it can disrupt the digital services those computers provide. So a flood or fire is a CB-recognized physical-security threat even though no person is involved.

exercise2.title: Xtensr Vulnerability Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 3 findings - Scenario 2A
exercise2.intro: You are on the physical-security team at Xtensr Research Labs, which has just acquired a smaller lab in the same town. Conduct a physical vulnerability assessment of three findings from the new lab. For each one, run the EK 2.2.A/B sign check (which named attack(s) the finding enables), state the mechanism and the CB risk level it should receive, and assign the high/moderate/low rating by name and EK. Advanced students also name the enrichment risk-driver sub-type. Vague 'be careful' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): sign check + mechanism + CB risk level (EK 2.2.C.2–C.4) for all 3 findings. Enrichment (optional): also name the risk-driver sub-type.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every finding
method: Identify the named physical attack(s) the finding enables — piggybacking, tailgating, shoulder surfing, dumpster diving, card cloning, power disruption, port attack — and cite the EK. Only claim what the finding shows.

State what an adversary could do with this weakness and how sensitive or valuable the exposed asset is. Physical access can bypass technical controls (EK 2.2.C.1), so weigh what is reachable once inside.
Step 3 - Assign the CB risk level
Rate the finding high (EK 2.2.C.2), moderate (EK 2.2.C.3), or low (EK 2.2.C.4) using asset value and likelihood of exploitation, and justify the grade — naming the level alone earns half credit.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment risk-driver sub-types (NOT required by the CED for Topic 2.2): Crown-jewel exposure = a sensitive system reachable without controlled access (drives HIGH). Foothold exposure = a noncritical asset that could be a pivot into other resources (drives MODERATE). Low-value/low-likelihood exposure = a cheap asset unlikely to be targeted (drives LOW). The skill is knowing WHICH driver applies, not labeling everything high.

scenarios.text: Scenario 2A — Xtensr's new lab. The server holding the company's research data sits in a room with NO lock, reached through an unmonitored hallway past a single badge door. The badge door also serves a busy loading dock and is propped open during deliveries.

key.signs: The propped, shared badge door enables piggybacking (EK 2.2.A.2) with a large delivery and tailgating (EK 2.2.A.3) during deliveries; the unlocked server room then exposes the research data (EK 2.2.B.4 steal/copy; EK 2.2.B.5 port attack or destruction).
key.mechanism: An adversary gets past the door socially or unnoticed, walks the unmonitored hallway, and reaches the research server directly. Physical access bypasses the server's technical controls (EK 2.2.C.1), so they could copy the research data, plug in a malware drive, or destroy the device. This is a sensitive system exposed without sufficiently restricted, controlled access. Risk level: HIGH (EK 2.2.C.2).
key.subtype: Crown-jewel exposure — a sensitive research system reachable without controlled access drives the HIGH rating.

scenarios.text: Past the controlled reception area sits a receptionist's PC that connects to the lab's internal wireless network and has exposed, accessible USB ports. The machine itself stores nothing sensitive, but any visitor waiting in the lobby can reach it.

key.signs: The exposed USB ports enable a port attack (EK 2.2.B.5) — a visitor could plug in a keylogger or malware-loaded drive — and the PC sits on the internal network, making it a pivot. No sensitive data is on the device itself.
key.mechanism: The PC holds nothing sensitive, so it is not a crown jewel, but it is a noncritical asset left unprotected in a way that could act as a foothold to gain initial access to other internal resources via the internal network. That is the textbook moderate case. Risk level: MODERATE (EK 2.2.C.3). Trap: rating it HIGH over-applies the framework — the device itself has no sensitive data; rating it LOW ignores the foothold into the network.
key.subtype: Foothold exposure — a noncritical, internet-connected asset that could pivot into other resources drives the MODERATE rating (EK 2.2.C.3).

scenarios.text: In a badge-access lab area, staff leave personal-use laptops on their desks during a shared lunch. The laptops hold no sensitive data, are not cabled to the desks, and the surrounding area is already behind controlled badge access. A junior teammate wants to flag this as the lab's top-priority HIGH risk.

key.signs: Unsecured laptops left out could in principle be grabbed, but they are in a badge-controlled area, hold no sensitive data, and are low value — so the named attacks (theft of a low-value asset) are present but the exposure is limited.
key.mechanism: The asset is of low value and, given the controlled badge access around it, the vulnerability is unlikely to be exploited and would yield little if it were. That is exactly the CED's low-risk definition. Risk level: LOW (EK 2.2.C.4). The junior teammate's HIGH flag is the planted trap: not every unsecured device is a top priority — high risk requires sensitive systems exposed without controlled access (EK 2.2.C.2), which this is not.
key.subtype: Low-value / low-likelihood exposure — a cheap asset in a controlled area, unlikely to be exploited, drives the LOW rating (EK 2.2.C.4). Calling it HIGH is the planted trap.

discussion.title: Securing the Space
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 4)
discussion.overview: Cybersecurity stops being purely digital the moment an attacker can walk through a door. Adversaries rarely defeat a lock — they manipulate the person holding it, or they exploit a building that was never designed to be questioned. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will examine why physical attacks succeed against people, debate which named attack is hardest to defend, and decide how to triage real physical vulnerabilities in the Xtensr labs without treating every weakness as a crisis.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. In the bell ringer, almost everyone's instinct is to hold the door for the person carrying the heavy box. Why is helpfulness so easy for an adversary to weaponize, and what would it actually take to make 'verify before you hold the door' a habit (not just 'be more careful')?

Question 2. Whose job is physical security, really — the individual employee who decides whether to hold a door, or the organization that designs the building and the badge system? Defend your split with one concrete example.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the physical-access exploits debrief
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The locked-down server  [EK 2.2.C.1]
items.text: A server has a strong password and sits behind a firewall, yet the security lead insists the unlocked room it sits in still matters. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, explain what physical access lets an adversary do that the password and firewall cannot stop (cite EK 2.2.B.5 for one concrete action).

items.label: Task B - Which named attack is hardest to defend?  [EK 2.2.A.2–A.6]
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: of the five named attacks — piggybacking, tailgating, shoulder surfing, dumpster diving, card cloning — which is hardest for a normal organization to actually defend against, and what is the realistic cost of getting it wrong? Record your group's ranking and one reason. Note which ones training fixes and which ones need hardware.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 4, ~9 min, after the Xtensr assessment table
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The triage call
items.text: Xtensr's assessment turned up three findings: an unlocked server room holding research data, a receptionist PC on the internal network with open USB ports, and badge-access laptops with no sensitive data left out at lunch. Design the triage: which single finding must be fixed FIRST and why, how do EK 2.2.C.2–C.4 justify giving the three findings DIFFERENT risk levels, and what would have to change about the laptops for them to jump from low to a higher level?
Our group's verdict (top-priority finding + one-sentence justification):
